items.label: Introduction
items.content:
# Introduction

Welcome to the official documentation for the **Titan Framework**. This guide is designed to help you set up, configure, and deploy your first application in minutes.

Titan is built on modern architecture principles, ensuring high availability and fault tolerance. Whether you are building a small microservice or a large-scale enterprise application, Titan provides the tools you need.

## Core Philosophy

1. **Simplicity First**: Minimal configuration required to start.
2. **Scalability**: Built-in support for horizontal scaling.
3. **Developer Experience**: Comprehensive CLI tools and hot-reloading.

> **Note**: This documentation assumes you have a basic understanding of TypeScript and Node.js.

items.label: Installation
items.content:
# Installation

Getting started with Titan is straightforward. Ensure you have **Node.js v18** or higher installed on your system.

### Step 1: Clone the Repository

```bash
git clone https://github.com/titan-framework/starter.git my-project
cd my-project
```

### Step 2: Install Dependencies

We recommend using `pnpm` for faster installation times, but `npm` works just as well.

```bash
pnpm install
# or
npm install
```

### Step 3: Environment Variables

Copy the example environment file to configure your local settings.

```bash
cp .env.example .env
```

Open the `.env` file and update the `DATABASE_URL` and `SECRET_KEY`.

items.label: Project Structure
items.content:
# Project Structure

Understanding the file structure is key to mastering the framework.

```
my-project/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ app/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ controllers/    # Route handlers
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ models/         # Database schemas
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ services/       # Business logic
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ config/
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ app.config.ts   # Main configuration
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ public/             # Static assets
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ tests/              # Test suites
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ package.json
```

### The `app` Directory

This is where the magic happens. All your application logic resides here. Keep your controllers lean by moving complex logic to **services**.

children.label: Authentication
Logging
children.content:
# Logging Middleware

Gain visibility into your application's request lifecycle. The logging middleware automatically captures request duration, status codes, and error stacks.

### Configuration

Enable logging globally in your `app.config.ts`.

```typescript
export default {
  logging: {
    level: 'debug',
    format: 'json', // or 'text'
    output: ['console', 'file']
  }
}
```

### Output Example

```json
{
  "level": "info",
  "method": "GET",
  "url": "/api/users",
  "status": 200,
  "duration": "12ms"
}
```

Docker
items.content:
# Docker Deployment

We provide a pre-configured `Dockerfile` optimized for production builds.

### Build the Image

```bash
docker build -t my-titan-app .
```

### Run the Container

```bash
docker run -p 3000:3000 -e DATABASE_URL=postgres://user:pass@db:5432/db my-titan-app
```

### Dockerfile

```dockerfile
FROM node:18-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci --only=production
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

FROM node:18-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /app /app
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "dist/main.js"]
```

items.label: Cloud Providers
items.content:
# Cloud Deployment

Titan is platform-agnostic and can be deployed to AWS, Google Cloud, Azure, or Vercel.

### Vercel (Serverless)

Simply connect your repository to Vercel. It will auto-detect the configuration.

### AWS EC2

Use PM2 to manage the process on your EC2 instance.

```bash
npm install -g pm2
pm2 start dist/main.js --name titan-app
```
